simple_explanation: The top part of your body containing the brain, eyes, nose, and mouth.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about headaches, wearing hats, or nodding.
natural_native_use: I have a splitting headache.
examples: She shook her head to say no.
Wear a helmet to protect your head.
hindi_confusion_correction: सिर = head, दिमाग (सोचने की क्षमता) = mind.

simple_explanation: The thread-like strands growing on a person's head or body.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about grooming, haircuts, or appearance.
natural_native_use: Her hair looks amazing today.
examples: He has dark brown hair.
I am getting my hair cut tomorrow.
common_mistakes: Saying 'hairs' when referring to all the hair on someone's head. 'Hair' is uncountable in this context.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मेरे बाल काले हैं' (plural), but in English it is 'My hair is black' (singular/uncountable).

simple_explanation: The front part of the head, from the forehead to the chin.
daily_life_use: Used to describe expressions, appearance, or washing up.
natural_native_use: She had a big smile on her face.
examples: I wash my face every morning.
He looked at her face closely.
hindi_confusion_correction: चेहरा = face. It can also mean to confront something ('face a problem').

simple_explanation: The organ of the body that gives you the ability to see.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about vision, seeing, or eye color.
natural_native_use: I got something in my eye.
examples: She has beautiful brown eyes.
Close your eyes and go to sleep.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the plural 's' when talking about both eyes.
hindi_confusion_correction: आँखें = eyes. Always use plural when referring to both.

simple_explanation: The organ used for hearing on the sides of the head.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about listening, hearing, or earaches.
natural_native_use: I have an ear infection.
examples: He whispered something in her ear.
Cover your ears, it's too loud.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Ear' starts with an 'ee' sound, 'Year' starts with a 'y' sound.

simple_explanation: The part of the face used for breathing and smelling.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about smells, breathing, or having a cold.
natural_native_use: I have a runny nose.
examples: This flower smells so good to my nose.
He got hit on the nose.
hindi_confusion_correction: नाक = nose.

simple_explanation: The opening in the face used for eating, speaking, and breathing.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about eating, talking, or oral hygiene.
natural_native_use: Don't speak with your mouth full.
examples: Put the food in your mouth.
She covered her mouth when she laughed.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the 'th' sound at the end.
hindi_confusion_correction: मुँह is mouth, but 'face' is चेहरा. Don't use 'mouth' when you mean 'face' (e.g., wash your face, not mouth).

simple_explanation: One of the hard white objects in the mouth used for biting and chewing.
daily_life_use: Used for dental hygiene and eating.
natural_native_use: I have a bad toothache.
examples: My tooth hurts a lot.
I lost my front tooth when I was a kid.
hindi_confusion_correction: One दांत is tooth. Multiple दांत are teeth.

simple_explanation: The soft organ inside the mouth used for tasting, swallowing, and speaking.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about taste, eating, or languages.
natural_native_use: The soup burned my tongue.
examples: He stuck his tongue out at the doctor.
English is not my mother tongue.
hindi_confusion_correction: जीभ = tongue. It also means language/dialect (e.g., mother tongue).

simple_explanation: The part of the body connecting the head to the shoulders.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about clothing, jewelry, or physical pain.
natural_native_use: I woke up with a pain in my neck.
examples: She wore a beautiful necklace around her neck.
He grabbed him by the neck.
hindi_confusion_correction: गला means throat (inside) or neck (outside). In English, 'neck' is outside, 'throat' is inside.

simple_explanation: The joint connecting the arm to the body.
daily_life_use: Used when carrying bags, expressing tiredness, or pain.
natural_native_use: My shoulders are tense.
emotional_tone: Neutral. Can imply burden ('weight on shoulders').
examples: He carried the heavy bag on his shoulder.
She shrugged her shoulders.
hindi_confusion_correction: कंधा = shoulder.

simple_explanation: The upper limb of the body, from the shoulder to the hand.
daily_life_use: Used for actions like lifting, hugging, or describing injuries.
natural_native_use: She crossed her arms.
examples: He broke his arm playing football.
She wrapped her arms around him.
common_mistakes: Using 'hand' instead of 'arm' to describe the whole limb.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'हाथ' means both arm and hand. In English, 'arm' is shoulder to wrist, and 'hand' is wrist to fingers.

simple_explanation: The end part of the arm, containing fingers and a thumb, used for holding things.
daily_life_use: Very common. Used for touching, holding, or gesturing.
natural_native_use: Hold my hand.
examples: She held the cup in her hand.
Please raise your hand to ask a question.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hand is only the part with fingers. The rest is the arm.

simple_explanation: One of the five separate parts at the end of the hand.
daily_life_use: Used when pointing, touching, or wearing rings.
natural_native_use: I cut my finger while cooking.
examples: He pointed his finger at the door.
She wore a ring on her ring finger.
hindi_confusion_correction: पैर की उंगली is 'toe', हाथ की उंगली is 'finger'.

simple_explanation: The front of the body between the neck and the stomach.
daily_life_use: Used regarding breathing, heart, or medical issues.
natural_native_use: I have chest pain.
examples: The doctor listened to my chest with a stethoscope.
He crossed his arms over his chest.
hindi_confusion_correction: सीना = chest.

simple_explanation: The organ in the body where food is digested.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about hunger, eating, or feeling sick.
natural_native_use: I have a stomach ache.
examples: Don't take this medicine on an empty stomach.
Eating too much junk food will upset your stomach.
hindi_confusion_correction: Pronounce it 'sta-mak', not 'sta-mach'.

simple_explanation: The rear part of the human body from the shoulders to the hips.
daily_life_use: Used for posture, carrying bags, or pain.
natural_native_use: I injured my lower back.
examples: Keep your back straight when you sit.
He carried the heavy box on his back.
hindi_confusion_correction: पीठ is 'back'. It also means 'behind' or 'return' in different contexts.

simple_explanation: One of the lower limbs of the body used for walking and standing.
daily_life_use: Used when walking, running, or sitting.
natural_native_use: He broke his leg skiing.
examples: I have been standing all day, my legs hurt.
She sat with her legs crossed.
hindi_confusion_correction: टांग = leg (thigh to ankle), पैर = foot (ankle downwards).

simple_explanation: The joint in the middle of the leg.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about bending, sitting, or joint pain.
natural_native_use: My knees hurt when I climb stairs.
examples: He fell and scraped his knee.
The doctor checked his left knee.
hindi_confusion_correction: The 'k' is completely silent. Just say 'नी'.

simple_explanation: The part of the leg that touches the ground when you stand.
daily_life_use: Used regarding walking, shoes, or kicking.
natural_native_use: I have a blister on my foot.
examples: I dropped a heavy book on my foot.
He goes to school on foot.
hindi_confusion_correction: One पैर is foot. Two पैर are feet.

simple_explanation: The hard white pieces that form the skeleton of the body.
daily_life_use: Used in medical situations, injuries, or describing thinness.
natural_native_use: Milk is good for your bones.
examples: The X-ray showed a broken bone.
Dogs love to chew on bones.
hindi_confusion_correction: हड्डी = bone.

simple_explanation: The red liquid that flows through the bodies of humans and animals.
daily_life_use: Used in injuries, medical tests, or hospitals.
natural_native_use: She faints at the sight of blood.
examples: The doctor took some blood for a test.
He lost a lot of blood in the accident.
hindi_confusion_correction: खून = blood.

simple_explanation: The organ in your chest that pumps blood through your body.
daily_life_use: Used for the physical organ and for emotions (love, kindness).
natural_native_use: He has a kind heart.
examples: Regular exercise is good for your heart.
She has a very kind heart.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hurt means 'चोट', Heart means 'दिल'. The pronunciation is different.

simple_explanation: The organ inside the head that controls thought, memory, and feeling.
daily_life_use: Used when talking about thinking, intelligence, or head injuries.
natural_native_use: The brain needs rest to function properly.
examples: The brain is the most complex organ in the body.
He is the brain behind this project (smartest person).
hindi_confusion_correction: दिमाग = brain (organ) or mind (abstract).

simple_explanation: The natural outer layer covering the body.
daily_life_use: Used for skincare, touching, injuries, or appearance.
natural_native_use: You need to protect your skin from the sun.
examples: She has very clear skin.
The burn left a mark on his skin.
common_mistakes: Saying 'skins' instead of 'skin' for general skin.
hindi_confusion_correction: चमड़ी = skin. Always singular when referring to a person's complexion.

simple_explanation: The condition of a person's body or mind, usually meaning free from illness.
daily_life_use: Used to describe general wellbeing, eating habits, or lifestyle.
natural_native_use: Smoking is bad for your health.
examples: He is in good health for his age.
Health is more important than wealth.
hindi_confusion_correction: Health = सेहत (Noun). Healthy = सेहतमंद (Adjective).

simple_explanation: Not healthy; suffering from a disease or illness.
daily_life_use: Used when feeling unwell, taking leave from work, or describing illness.
natural_native_use: I am calling in sick today.
examples: I can't come to school, I am sick.
The soup made me feel sick.
common_mistakes: Using 'sick' for long-term serious diseases (we usually use 'ill' for that, though 'sick' is acceptable).
hindi_confusion_correction: बीमार = sick. 'Ill' is more formal.
formal_informal_note: Informal and standard. 'Ill' is more formal.

simple_explanation: An abnormally high body temperature, usually accompanied by shivering and headache.
daily_life_use: Used when sick, checking temperature, or visiting a doctor.
natural_native_use: His fever broke during the night.
examples: You should rest if you have a fever.
The child's fever went down after medicine.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am fever' instead of 'I have a fever'.
hindi_confusion_correction: मुझे बुखार है = I HAVE a fever. Not 'I am fever'.

simple_explanation: A common illness affecting the nose and throat, causing sneezing and coughing.
daily_life_use: Used to describe weather OR a very common viral sickness.
natural_native_use: I have a terrible cold right now.
examples: Wear a jacket or you'll catch a cold.
She is in bed with a cold.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I feel cold' (मुझे ठंड लग रही है) when meaning 'I have a cold' (मुझे जुकाम है).
hindi_confusion_correction: I am cold = मुझे ठंड लग रही है। I have a cold = मुझे जुकाम है।

simple_explanation: To force air out of your lungs with a sudden sharp sound, or the act of doing this.
daily_life_use: Used when sick, having a tickle in the throat, or clearing the throat.
natural_native_use: Cover your mouth when you cough.
examples: He has been coughing all night.
I need to buy some cough syrup.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing as 'coog' or 'co-gh'. It rhymes with 'off'.
hindi_confusion_correction: खांसी (Noun) = a cough. खांसना (Verb) = to cough.

simple_explanation: The physical feeling caused by disease, injury, or something that hurts.
daily_life_use: Used to describe discomfort anywhere in the body.
natural_native_use: I feel a sharp pain in my chest.
examples: The medicine will reduce the pain.
He cried out in pain.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'pain' with 'hurt' (verb). 'My leg is paining' is common in Indian English but native speakers say 'My leg hurts'.
hindi_confusion_correction: मुझे दर्द हो रहा है = I am in pain / My leg hurts. Avoid 'My leg is paining'.

simple_explanation: A substance used for treating disease or illness.
daily_life_use: Used when going to the pharmacy, doctor, or treating illness at home.
natural_native_use: I need to buy some medicine for my headache.
examples: You should take this medicine twice a day.
Modern medicine has cured many diseases.
hindi_confusion_correction: दवा खाना = Take medicine. In English, we do NOT 'eat' medicine.

simple_explanation: A person whose job is to treat people who are sick or injured.
daily_life_use: Used when seeking medical help.
natural_native_use: The doctor told me to rest for a week.
examples: She wants to study medicine and become a doctor.
Call a doctor immediately!
hindi_confusion_correction: डॉक्टर = doctor. Both languages use the same word heavily.

simple_explanation: A place where people who are sick or injured are given medical care.
daily_life_use: Used in emergencies, surgeries, or serious illnesses.
natural_native_use: She was taken to the hospital by ambulance.
examples: The hospital is near the city center.
He spent three days in the hospital.
hindi_confusion_correction: अस्पताल = hospital.

simple_explanation: The resting state in which the body is not active and the mind is unconscious.
daily_life_use: Very common. Discussing rest, nighttime, or tiredness.
natural_native_use: I need to get some sleep.
examples: Babies need a lot of sleep.
I slept for eight hours last night.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am sleep' instead of 'I am sleepy' or 'I am sleeping'.
hindi_confusion_correction: मुझे नींद आ रही है = I am sleepy. मैं सो रहा हूँ = I am sleeping.

simple_explanation: In need of sleep or rest; weary.
daily_life_use: Used after a long day of work or physical activity.
natural_native_use: I'm too tired to cook tonight.
examples: He was very tired after running the marathon.
I am tired of waiting for the bus.
common_mistakes: Saying 'tiring' instead of 'tired' to describe one's own feeling.
hindi_confusion_correction: मैं थका हुआ हूँ = I am tired. यह काम थका देने वाला है = This work is tiring.

simple_explanation: To take air into the lungs and let it out.
daily_life_use: Used regarding health, relaxation, or exercise.
natural_native_use: I can't breathe in this smoke.
examples: The doctor told me to breathe deeply.
Fish breathe through their gills.
hindi_confusion_correction: सांस लेना = Breathe (Verb). सांस = Breath (Noun).

simple_explanation: An illness that affects a person, animal, or plant.
daily_life_use: Used for serious or long-term health issues.
natural_native_use: Scientists are trying to find a cure for the disease.
examples: Malaria is a dangerous disease.
He suffers from a rare skin disease.
common_mistakes: Using 'disease' for small things like a cold (we use 'illness' or just 'sickness' for that).
hindi_confusion_correction: रोग = Disease (serious/specific). बीमारी = Illness/Sickness (general).

simple_explanation: Lacking physical strength and energy.
daily_life_use: Used when recovering from illness, lifting heavy things, or feeling tired.
natural_native_use: My legs feel weak after the long walk.
examples: The illness left him feeling very weak.
She was too weak to stand up.
hindi_confusion_correction: कमज़ोर = weak. सप्ताह = week. Both are pronounced exactly the same.

simple_explanation: Having great physical power and ability.
daily_life_use: Used regarding health, fitness, or moving heavy items.
natural_native_use: He has a strong immune system.
examples: You need to be strong to lift this box.
Eating healthy food makes you strong.
hindi_confusion_correction: मज़बूत = strong.
